title: Enzyme Commission number

infobox.value: Reaction catalyzed | Typical reaction | Enzyme example(s) with trivial name

(**EC number**) is a [numerical classification](/wiki/Numbering_scheme "Numbering scheme") scheme for [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme"), based on the [chemical reactions](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction") they [catalyze](/wiki/Catalysis "Catalysis").[[1]](#cite_note-isbn0-12-227164-5-1) As a system of **enzyme nomenclature**, every EC number is associated with a recommended name for the corresponding enzyme-catalyzed reaction.

[*Enzyme nomenclature 1992: recommendations of the Nomenclature Committee of the International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology on the nomenclature and classification of enzymes*](https://archive.org/details/enzymenomenclatu0000inte_d6c2). From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Chemical numbering scheme

Not to be confused with [European Community number](/wiki/European_Community_number "European Community number").

The **Enzyme Commission number** (**EC number**) is a [numerical classification](/wiki/Numbering_scheme "Numbering scheme") scheme for [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme"), based on the [chemical reactions](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction") they [catalyze](/wiki/Catalysis "Catalysis").[[1]](#cite_note-isbn0-12-227164-5-1) As a system of **enzyme nomenclature**, every EC number is associated with a recommended name for the corresponding enzyme-catalyzed reaction.

EC numbers do not specify enzymes but enzyme-catalyzed reactions. If different enzymes (for instance from different organisms) catalyze the same reaction, then they receive the same EC number.[[2]](#cite_note-expasy-2) Furthermore, through [convergent evolution](/wiki/Convergent_evolution "Convergent evolution"), completely different [protein folds](/wiki/Protein_fold_class "Protein fold class") can catalyze an identical reaction (these are sometimes called [non-homologous isofunctional enzymes](/wiki/Non-homologous_isofunctional_enzymes "Non-homologous isofunctional enzymes"))[[3]](#cite_note-pmid20433725-3) and therefore would be assigned the same EC number. By contrast, [UniProt](/wiki/UniProt "UniProt") identifiers uniquely specify a protein by its amino acid sequence.[[4]](#cite_note-pmid14681372-4)

## Format of number

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Enzyme_Commission_number&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Format of number")]

Every enzyme code consists of the letters "EC" followed by four numbers separated by periods. Those numbers represent a progressively finer classification of the enzyme. Preliminary EC numbers exist and have an 'n' as part of the fourth (serial) digit (e.g. EC 3.5.1.n3).[[2]](#cite_note-expasy-2)

For example, the [tripeptide aminopeptidases](/wiki/Tripeptide_aminopeptidase "Tripeptide aminopeptidase") have the code "EC 3.4.11.4", whose components indicate the following groups of enzymes:

* *EC 3* enzymes are hydrolases (enzymes that use [water](/wiki/Water "Water") to break up some other molecule)
* *EC 3.4* are hydrolases that act on [peptide bonds](/wiki/Peptide_bond "Peptide bond")
* *EC 3.4.11* are those hydrolases that cleave off the amino-terminal [amino acid](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") from a [polypeptide](/wiki/Polypeptide "Polypeptide")
* *EC 3.4.11.4* are those that cleave off the amino-terminal end from a [tripeptide](/wiki/Tripeptide "Tripeptide")

## Top level codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Enzyme_Commission_number&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Top level codes")]

NB:The enzyme classification number is different from the 'FORMAT NUMBER'

Top-level EC numbers[[5]](#cite_note-moss-5)

| Class | Reaction catalyzed | Typical reaction | Enzyme example(s) with trivial name |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [EC 1](/wiki/List_of_EC_numbers_(EC_1) "List of EC numbers (EC 1)") *[Oxidoreductases](/wiki/Oxidoreductase "Oxidoreductase")* | [Oxidation](/wiki/Oxidation "Oxidation")/reduction reactions; transfer of H and O atoms or [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron") from one substance to another | AH + B → A + BH (**reduced**)  A + O → AO (**oxidized**) | [Dehydrogenase](/wiki/Dehydrogenase "Dehydrogenase"), [oxidase](/wiki/Oxidase "Oxidase") |
| [EC 2](/wiki/List_of_EC_numbers_(EC_2) "List of EC numbers (EC 2)") *[Transferases](/wiki/Transferase "Transferase")* | Transfer of a [functional group](/wiki/Functional_group "Functional group") from one substance to another. The group may be methyl-, acyl-, amino- or phosphate group | AB + C → A + BC | [Transaminase](/wiki/Transaminase "Transaminase"), [kinase](/wiki/Kinase "Kinase") |
| [EC 3](/wiki/List_of_EC_numbers_(EC_3) "List of EC numbers (EC 3)") *[Hydrolases](/wiki/Hydrolase "Hydrolase")* | Formation of two products from a substrate by [hydrolysis](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis") | AB + H2O → AOH + BH | [Lipase](/wiki/Lipase "Lipase"), [amylase](/wiki/Amylase "Amylase"), [peptidase](/wiki/Peptidase "Peptidase"), [phosphatase](/wiki/Phosphatase "Phosphatase") |
| [EC 4](/wiki/List_of_EC_numbers_(EC_4) "List of EC numbers (EC 4)") *[Lyases](/wiki/Lyase "Lyase")* | Non-hydrolytic addition or removal of groups from substrates. C-C, C-N, C-O or C-S bonds may be cleaved | RCOCOOH → RCOH + CO2 or [X-A+B-Y] → [A=B + X-Y] | [Decarboxylase](/wiki/Decarboxylase "Decarboxylase") |
| [EC 5](/wiki/List_of_EC_numbers_(EC_5) "List of EC numbers (EC 5)") *[Isomerases](/wiki/Isomerase "Isomerase")* | Intramolecule rearrangement, i.e. [isomerization](/wiki/Isomer "Isomer") changes within a single molecule | ABC → BCA | [Isomerase](/wiki/Isomerase "Isomerase"), [mutase](/wiki/Mutase "Mutase") |
| [EC 6](/wiki/List_of_EC_numbers_(EC_6) "List of EC numbers (EC 6)") *[Ligases](/wiki/Ligase "Ligase")* | Join together two molecules by synthesis of new C-O, C-S, C-N or C-C [bonds](/wiki/Covalent_bond "Covalent bond") with simultaneous breakdown of [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") | X + Y + ATP → XY + ADP + Pi | [Synthetase](/wiki/Synthetase "Synthetase") |
| [EC 7](/wiki/List_of_EC_numbers_(EC_7) "List of EC numbers (EC 7)") *[Translocases](/wiki/Translocase "Translocase")* | Catalyse the movement of ions or molecules across membranes or their separation within membranes | AX + Bside 1 = A + X + Bside 2 | [Transporter](/wiki/Membrane_transport_protein "Membrane transport protein"), [ornithine translocase](/wiki/Ornithine_translocase "Ornithine translocase"), [sodium–potassium pump](/wiki/Sodium%E2%80%93potassium_pump "Sodium–potassium pump") |

## Reaction similarity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Enzyme_Commission_number&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Reaction similarity")]

Similarity between enzymatic reactions can be calculated by using bond changes, reaction centres or substructure metrics (formerly EC-BLAST], now the EMBL-EBI Enzyme Portal).[[6]](#cite_note-asad-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Enzyme_Commission_number&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

Before the development of the EC number system, enzymes were named in an arbitrary fashion, and names like [old yellow enzyme](/wiki/Old_yellow_enzyme "Old yellow enzyme") and [malic enzyme](/wiki/Malic_enzyme "Malic enzyme") that give little or no clue as to what reaction was catalyzed were in common use. Most of these names have fallen into disuse, though a few, especially proteolytic enzymes with very low specificity, such as [pepsin](/wiki/Pepsin "Pepsin") and [papain](/wiki/Papain "Papain"), are still used, as rational classification on the basis of specificity has been very difficult.

By the 1950s the chaos was becoming intolerable, and after Hoffman-Ostenhof[[7]](#cite_note-7) and Dixon and Webb[[8]](#cite_note-8) had proposed somewhat similar schemes for classifying enzyme-catalyzed reactions, the International Congress of Biochemistry in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") set up the Commission on Enzymes under the chairmanship of [Malcolm Dixon](/wiki/Malcolm_Dixon_(biochemist) "Malcolm Dixon (biochemist)") in 1955. The first version was published in 1961, and the Enzyme Commission was dissolved at that time, though its name lives on in the term *EC Number*. The current sixth edition, published by the [International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology](/wiki/International_Union_of_Biochemistry_and_Molecular_Biology "International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology") in 1992 as the last version published as a printed book, contains 3196 different enzymes. Supplements 1-4 were published 1993–1999. Subsequent supplements have been published electronically, at the website of the Nomenclature Committee of the International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology.[[5]](#cite_note-moss-5) In August 2018, the [IUBMB](/wiki/IUBMB "IUBMB") modified the system by adding the top-level EC 7 category containing translocases.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
